ex.u: بھجن کرنا

senses.g: the act of carrying, bearing, supporting, maintaining, sustaining, nourishing, filling, satisfying, paying

بھرنا.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀪𑀭𑀇 (bharaï), from Sanskrit भर॑ति (bhárati), from Proto-Indo-Iranian *bʰárati, from Proto-Indo-European *bʰéreti, from *bʰer-.

senses.g: devotion; bhakti; reverence; devoutness; adoration, fidelity

بھکتی.e: Learned borrowing from Sanskrit भक्ति (bhaktí). Related to بھکت (bhakt), بھَجن (bhajan).

senses.g: Bhakkar (a city in Bhakkar district, Punjab, Pakistan)

ex.u: کابل میں امریکی سفارت خانے نے امریکی شہریوں کو خبردار کیا ہے کہ وہ حکومتی ہدایت کے بغیر ایئر پورٹ کا رُخ نہ کریں جب کہ برطانوی وزارتِ دفاع نے تصدیق کی ہے کہ کابل ایئر پورٹ کے باہر بھگدڑ مچنے کے باعث سات افغان باشندے ہلاک ہوئے ہیں
ex.r: kābul mẽ amrīkī sifārat-xāne ne amrīkī śahriyõ ko xabardār kiyā hai ki vo hukūmatī hidāyat ke baġair e'irporṭ kā rux na karẽ jab ki bartānavī vizārat-e-dafā' ne tasdīq kiya hai ki kābul e'irporṭ ke bāhar bhagdauṛ macne ke bā'is sāt afġān bāśande halāk hu'e ha͠i
ex.e: The American embassy in Kabul has warned American citizens not to head to the airport without government instruction as the British Ministry of Defense has confirmed that seven Afghan residents were killed in a stampede.

بھگدڑ.e: From بھاگنا (bhāgnā, “to run away, to flee”) + دوڑنا (dauṛnā, “to run”).

ex.u: وہ بھگوان سے دعا کر رہی تھی۔
ex.r: vo bhagvān se du'ā kar rahī thī
ex.e: She was saying a prayer to God.

senses.g: Bhalwal (a city in Sargodha district, Punjab, Pakistan)

بھنڈی.e: From Sanskrit भिण्डा (bhiṇḍā) + Middle Indo-Aryan -𑀇𑀓𑀸- (-ikā-). Cognate with Punjabi بھنڈی (bhnḍī), Gujarati ભીંડો (bhī̃ḍo), and Marathi भेंडी (bheṇḍī).

بھنور.e: Inherited from Middle Hindi بھنور (bhnvr /⁠bhãvar⁠/) (c. 1658), from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀪𑀫𑀭 (bhamara), from Sanskrit भ्रमर (bhramará, “large black bee; spinning top”).

senses.g: an ugly man (i.e., a man of ghastly countenance)

بھوت.e: Borrowed from Sanskrit भू॒त (bhūtá), from Proto-Indo-Iranian *bʰuHtás, from Proto-Indo-European *bʰuH- + *-tós.

senses.g: ground, floor, surface of the Earth
an ugly woman (i.e., a woman of ghastly countenance)

بھوتنی.e: From بھوت (bhūt) + Sanskrit -इनी (-inī, Sanskrit feminine doer suffix) / + ـنی (-nī, “-ess”).

senses.g: Bhutan (a country in South Asia, in the Himalayas)

ex.u: بھور سمے تک جس نے ہمیں باہم الجھائے رکھا / وہ البیلی ریشم ایسی بات گزر گئی جاناں

بھور.e: Inherited from Sanskrit *भोला (*bholā, “dawn”). Cognate with Bengali ভোর (bhōr), Gujarati ભોર (bhor), Nepali भोर (bhor), Punjabi ਭੋਰ (bhor) / بھور (bhor), and Odia ଭୋର (bhora).

بھورا.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit *𑀪𑀽𑀭 (*bhūra) + Middle Indo-Aryan -𑀓- (-ka-), from Sanskrit *भ्रूर (*bhrūra), from Proto-Indo-Iranian *bʰruHrás, from Proto-Indo-European *bʰruH-ró-s, from the root *bʰerH-. Cognate with Punjabi بھورا (bhūrā), Gujarati ભૂરું (bhūrũ), Marathi भुरे (bhure), Classical Persian بور (bōr, būr, “brown, blonde”), Ossetian бур (bur, “yellow”), Northern Kurdish bor (“grey”), Yidgha وور (vūr, “pale red”) and Baluchi بور (bōr, “bay horse”). Compare Sanskrit बभ्रु (bábhru, babhrú, “brown”) from the same root.

Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit *𑀪𑀽𑀭 (*bhūra) + Middle Indo-Aryan -𑀓- (-ka-), from Sanskrit *भ्रूर (*bhrūra), from Proto-Indo-Iranian *bʰruHrás, from Proto-Indo-European *bʰruH-ró-s, from the root *bʰerH-. Cognate with Punjabi بھورا (bhūrā), Gujarati ભૂરું (bhūrũ), Marathi भुरे (bhure), Classical Persian بور (bōr, būr, “brown, blonde”), Ossetian бур (bur, “yellow”), Northern Kurdish bor (“grey”), Yidgha وور (vūr, “pale red”) and Baluchi بور (bōr, “bay horse”). Compare Sanskrit बभ्रु (bábhru, babhrú, “brown”) from the same root.

ex.u: کْیَا تُمْہیں بُھوک لَگِی ہَے ؟

بھوک.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀩𑀼𑀪𑀼𑀓𑁆𑀔𑀸 (bubhukkhā), from Sanskrit बुभुक्षा (bubhukṣā). Cognate with Punjabi ਭੁੱਖ (bhukkha)/بُھکّھ (bhukkh), Odia ଭୋକ (bhoka), Marathi भूक (bhūk), Gujarati ભૂખ (bhūkh), Bengali ভুখ (bhukh). Doublet of بُبُھکْشَا (bubhukṣā).

بھوکا.e: Inherited from Prakrit *𑀩𑀼𑀳𑀼𑀓𑁆𑀔𑀅 (*buhukkhaa), from Sanskrit *बुभुक्षक (*bubhukṣaka, “desiring to eat”), a back-formation from बुभुक्षति (bubhukṣati, “to desire to eat”) + -अक (-aka, “doer”). By surface analysis, بُھوک (bhūk, “hunger”) + ـا (-ā). Cognate with Braj भूखौ (bhūkhau).

Inherited from Prakrit *𑀩𑀼𑀳𑀼𑀓𑁆𑀔𑀅 (*buhukkhaa), from Sanskrit *बुभुक्षक (*bubhukṣaka, “desiring to eat”), a back-formation from बुभुक्षति (bubhukṣati, “to desire to eat”) + -अक (-aka, “doer”). By surface analysis, بُھوک (bhūk, “hunger”) + ـا (-ā). Cognate with Braj भूखौ (bhūkhau).

senses.g: inflection of بُھوکا (bhūkā, “hungry; starved”): — direct masculine plural
inflection of بُھوکا (bhūkā, “hungry; starved”): — oblique masculine singular/plural

بھولا.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀪𑁄𑀮 (bhola), *𑀪𑁄𑀮𑁆𑀮 (*bhŏlla), from Proto-New Indo-Aryan *𑀪𑁄𑀮 (*bhola).

senses.g: Bhubaneshwar (a city, the capital of Odisha, India)

بھی.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀯𑀺 (vi), 𑀧𑀺 (pi), 𑀅𑀯𑀺 (avi), from Sanskrit अपि (api).

senses.g: term of address for any male older than oneself

بھیا.e: PIE word *bʰréh₂tēr A diminutive of بھائی (bhā'ī, “brother”), from Sanskrit भ्रातृ (bhrātṛ), ultimately from Proto-Indo-European *bʰréh₂tēr (“brother”).

ex.u: کل میں تمھیں ایک خط بھیجونگا
ex.e: I will send you a letter tomorrow.

بھیجنا.e: Perhaps from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀯𑀺𑀲𑀚𑁆𑀚𑀤𑀺 (visajjadi), from Sanskrit विसर्जयति (visarjayati, “to send, utter”). Turner disagrees, and reconstructs Ashokan Prakrit *𑀪𑁂𑀚𑁆𑀚𑀢𑀺 (*bhejjati), possibly from a Sanskrit अभि-अज्यते (abhi-ajyate, “to be led to”). The first etymology is slightly problematic, but the changes from s to h in Prakrit is not uncommon and so it is plausible. Tedesco gives the development visajj > *vihajj > undergoing metathesis *vhiajj > *vhejj. Cognate with Gujarati ભેજવું (bhejvũ), Punjabi ਭੇਜਣਾ (bhejṇā) / بهیجنا (bhejṇā), Nepali भेज्नु (bhejnu), Odia ଭେଜିବା (bhejibā), Maithili भेजब (bhejab).

بھید.e: Borrowed from Sanskrit भेद (bheda). First attested in c. 1611 as Middle Hindi بھید (bhyd).

بھیڑ.e: Inherited from Prakrit *𑀪𑁂𑀟 (*bheḍa), from Sanskrit भेड्र (bheḍra), from a substrate, possibly Munda. Cognate with Bengali ভেড়া (bheṛa), Bhojpuri भेड़ा (bhēṛā), Gujarati ભેડ (bheḍ), and Punjabi ਭੇਡ (bheḍ).
Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit *𑀪𑀻𑀟 (*bhīḍa), from Ashokan Prakrit *𑀪𑀺𑀝𑁆 (*bhiṭ). Cognate with Bengali ভিড় (bhiṛ), Gujarati ભીડ (bhīḍ), Marathi भीड (bhīḍ), and Punjabi ਭੀੜ (bhīṛ).

ex.u: دہشتگردوں نے اہلکاروں کے بھس میں، سرکاری عمارت میں گھس گئے۔
ex.r: dahśatgardõ ne ahlkārõ ke bhes mẽ, sarkārī imārat mẽ ghus gaye.
ex.e: The terrorists sneaked into the government building in the guise of officials.

بھیس.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀯𑁂𑀲 (vesa), from Sanskrit वेष (vēṣa).

بھیک.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀪𑀺𑀓𑁆𑀔𑀸 (bhikkhā), from Sanskrit भिक्षा (bhikṣā).

بھیک مانگنا.e: From بھیک (bhīk, “begging”) + مانگنا (māṅgnā, “to ask”), literally “to ask for begging”.

senses.g: alternative form of بَہْنوئِی (bahnoī, “brother-in-law, sister's husband”)
a type of fish found in the Indian subcontinent
the name of a special type of grass

بھینس.e: First attested in c. 1503 as Middle Hindi بھینس (bhyns /⁠bhē̃ns⁠/), inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀫𑀳𑀺𑀲𑀻 (mahisī), from Sanskrit महिष (mahiṣa). Cognate with Nepali भैंसी (bha͠isī), Gujarati ભેંસ (bhẽs), Marathi म्हैस (mhais), Punjabi مھینس (mhaiṉs).

senses.g: The third letter of the Urdu alphabet, written in the Arabic script; preceded by ب and followed by ت.
inflection of پانا (pānā): — second-person singular intimate present imperative
a class of Brahmans specializing in teaching scriptures,
pyjamas (loose-fitting trousers worn by both sexes)

پاجامہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian پَاجَامَه (pājāma, “trousers, drawers”).

پاخانہ.e: From پا (pā, “foot(rest)”) + خانہ (xāna, “site, house”). Compare Bengali পায়খানা (paẏkhana).

پاد.e: Inherited from Sanskrit पर्द (parda), from Proto-Indo-European *perd-.

پاد گرم ہونا.e: Literally, “to have one's fart become hot”.

پادری.e: Borrowed from Portuguese padre, from Old Galician-Portuguese padre (“father”), from Latin pater, patrem (“father”), from Proto-Italic *patēr, from Proto-Indo-European *ph₂tḗr (“father”). Doublet of فادر (fādar).

پارچہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian پَارْچَه (pārča), from پَارَه (pāra) + diminutive suffix چَه (ča).

پارسی.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀧𑀸𑀭𑀲 (pārasa), from Sanskrit पारसि (pārasi), पारसिक (pārasika), from Middle Persian 𐭯𐭠𐭫𐭮𐭩' (pʾlsy /Pārs/), from Old Persian 𐎱𐎠𐎼𐎿 (Pārsa). Also influenced by Classical Persian پَارْسِی (pārsī). Cognate with Bengali পারসি (parśi), Gujarati પારસી (pārsī), Marathi पारशी (pārśī), Punjabi ਪਾਰਸੀ (pārsī) / پَارْسِی (pārsī). Doublet of پَارَس (pāras), فَارْسی (fārsī), and فَارَس (fāras).

Inherited from Prakrit 𑀧𑀸𑀭𑀲 (pārasa), from Sanskrit पारसि (pārasi), पारसिक (pārasika), from Middle Persian 𐭯𐭠𐭫𐭮𐭩' (pʾlsy /Pārs/), from Old Persian 𐎱𐎠𐎼𐎿 (Pārsa). Also influenced by Classical Persian پَارْسِی (pārsī). Cognate with Bengali পারসি (parśi), Gujarati પારસી (pārsī), Marathi पारशी (pārśī), Punjabi ਪਾਰਸੀ (pārsī) / پَارْسِی (pārsī). Doublet of پَارَس (pāras), فَارْسی (fārsī), and فَارَس (fāras).

Inherited from Prakrit 𑀧𑀸𑀭𑀲 (pārasa), from Sanskrit पारसि (pārasi), पारसिक (pārasika), from Middle Persian 𐭯𐭠𐭫𐭮𐭩' (pʾlsy /Pārs/), from Old Persian 𐎱𐎠𐎼𐎿 (Pārsa). Also influenced by Classical Persian پَارْسِی (pārsī). Cognate with Bengali পারসি (parśi), Gujarati પારસી (pārsī), Marathi पारशी (pārśī), Punjabi ਪਾਰਸੀ (pārsī) / پَارْسِی (pārsī). Doublet of پَارَس (pāras), فَارْسی (fārsī), and فَارَس (fāras).

senses.g: a female given name, Parvati, from Sanskrit, of Hindu usage
portion, bit, piece, slip, scrap, fragment
juz; any of thirty parts of varying length into which the Qur'an is sometimes divided

ex.u: رَؤُف پاریکھ

senses.g: anklet, an ornament worn on the feet or ankle

ex.u:
اس ریشمی پازیب کی جھنکار کے صدقے
جس نے یہ پہنائی ہے اس دلدار کے صدقے
ex.r:
is reśmī pāzeb kī jhaṅkār ke sadqe
 jis ne ye pahnāī hai us dildār ke sadqe

پازیب.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian پا زیب (pāzīb, pāzeb, literally “foot adornment”). First attested in c. 1713.

پاس.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀧𑀲𑁆𑀲 (passa), 𑀧𑀸𑀲 (pāsa), from Sanskrit पार्श्वे (pārśve). Cognate with Marathi पासून (pāsūn), Gujarati પાસે (pāse).
Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀧𑀲𑁆𑀲 (passa), 𑀧𑀸𑀲 (pāsa), from Sanskrit पार्श्वे (pārśve). Cognate with Marathi पासून (pāsūn), Gujarati પાસે (pāse).

پاستا.e: Borrowed from Persian پاستا (pâstâ) or English pasta, both from Italian pasta, ultimately from Ancient Greek παστά (pastá).

پاک.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian پَاک (pāk). First attested in c. 1265 as Old Hindi پاک (pak /⁠pāk⁠/).

ex.u: پاک فَوج

پاک.e: Shortening of پَاکِسْتَان (pākistān), itself from پاک (pāk) + ـِسْتَان (-istān).

senses.g: Pakpattan (a city in Pakpattan district, Punjab, Pakistan)
Pakistan (a country in South Asia; official name: اسلامی جمہوریہ پاکستان (islāmī jamhūriya-e pākistān, “Islamic Republic of Pakistan”); capital: اسلام آباد (islām-ābād, “Islamabad”))

پاکستان.e: Coined by Pakistani politician Chuhdary Rahmat Ali in 1933 as an acronym of Punjab, "Afghania", Kashmir, Sindh, and Balochistan. Alternatively, also analyzed as پاک (pāk, “pure”) + ـستان (-stān, “land”).

senses.g: crazy, mad, insane (ie. someone who has lost their mind)

ex.u: پاگَل ہو گَئے ہو کیا؟
وہ کِتابوں کے بارے میں بَڑا پاگَل ہَے
ex.r: vo kitābõ ke bāre mẽ baṛā pāgal hai

ex.u: وہ اُس لَڑکی کے بارے میں پاگَل ہَے
ex.r: vo us laṛkī ke bāre mẽ pāgal hai

ex.u: میرا بیٹا پاگَل ہَے
اُس گھر میں ایک پاگَل لَڑکی رَہتی ہَے
ex.e: A possessed girl lives in that house

senses.g: a male given name from English, equivalent to English Paul

پالک.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀧𑀸𑀮𑀓𑁆𑀓𑀸 (pālakkā), from Sanskrit पालक्या (pālakyā). Cognate with Gujarati પાલક (pālak).

senses.g: Palam (a neighborhood of South West Delhi district, Delhi, India)
Palam (a town and tehsil in Parbhani district, Maharashtra, India)

پالم.e: Uncertain. Perhaps inherited from Prakrit 𑀧𑀸𑀮𑀁𑀩 (pālaṃba, “neck ornament; long”), from Sanskrit प्रालम्ब (prālamba). According to a folk etymology, a blend of پَانِی (pānī) + عَالَم ('ālam).

پالنا.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀧𑀸𑀮𑁂𑀤𑀺 (pāledi), from Sanskrit पालयति (pāláyati). The noun is from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀧𑀸𑀮𑀡 (pālaṇa), from Sanskrit पालन (pālana), from the same verb.

پانا.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀧𑀸𑀯𑀇 (pāvai), from Sanskrit प्रापयति (prāpayati, “to cause to reach, attain, obtain”), from प्राप् (prāp, “to reach, attain, obtain”), a compound of प्र- (pra-, prepositional prefix) + आप् (āp, “to reach, obtain”).

پانچ.e: Inherited from Old Hindi पंच (paṃca), पांच (pāṃca), from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀧𑀁𑀘 (paṃca), from Sanskrit पञ्च (páñca).

senses.g: Pandavas (used to collectively refer to the five Pandavas)
a wedge to keep the door from flying open

پانی.e: Inherited from Old Hindi پانی (pānī), from Apabhramsa पाणिउ (pāṇiu), from Prakrit पाणीअ (pāṇīa), from Sanskrit पानीय (pānīya, “water, drink”).

پاؤں.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀧𑀸𑀅 (pāa), from Sanskrit पाद (pā́da) / पद् (pád, “foot”), from Proto-Indo-Aryan *pā́ts, from Proto-Indo-Iranian *pā́ts, and from Proto-Indo-European *pṓds.

پائجامہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian پایجامه (pāyjāma), archaic form of پاجامه (pājāma).

senses.g: footrest, footboard, running board (any platform holding the feet)

پایدان.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian پایدان (pāydān), from پای (pāy, “foot”) + دان (dān, “holder”).

پایل.e: Inherited from Sanskrit *पादल (*pādala). Cognate with Gujarati પાળું (pāḷũ).

senses.g: foot or leg of an object

ex.u: آپ کا پتا کیا ہے؟

پتا.e: Inherited from Prakrit पत्तिअ (pattia), from Sanskrit प्रत्याययति (pratyāyayati, “convinces”).
Borrowed from Sanskrit पितृ (pitṛ), from Proto-Indo-Iranian *pHtā́, ultimately from Proto-Indo-European *ph₂tḗr.

پتر.e: Borrowed from Sanskrit पुत्र (putrá). Doublet of پُوت (pūt) and پِسَر (pisar).

پتلا.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀧𑀢𑁆𑀢𑀮 (pattala, “thin”), from Sanskrit पत्त्रल (pattralá, “leafy”). Cognate with Gujarati પાતળું (pātaḷũ), Marathi पातळ (pātaḷ), Odia ପାତଳ (pātaḷa), Bengali পাতলা (patola), Assamese পাতল (patol).

ex.u: مجھے کالی پتلون سب سے زیادہ پسند ہے۔
ex.r: mujhē kāli patlūn sab se zyāda pasand hai.
ex.e: I like black pants the most.

پتلون.e: Borrowed from English pantaloon, borrowed from French pantalon, borrowed from Italian Pantalone, from Ancient Greek Παντελεήμων (Panteleḗmōn), from πᾶς (pâs) + ἐλεήμων (eleḗmōn).

پتنگ.e: Borrowed from Sanskrit पतङ्ग (pataṅga, “the flier, flying insect e.g. moth”).

پتی.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀧𑀢𑁆𑀢𑀺𑀆 (pattiā), from Sanskrit पत्त्रिका (pattrikā), from पत्त्र (pattra) + -इका (-ikā).
Learned borrowing from Sanskrit पति (páti), from Proto-Indo-Iranian *pátiš, from Proto-Indo-European *pótis.

senses.g: large stewpot, big pot for cooking,

پتھ.e: Borrowed from Sanskrit पथ (pathá), from Proto-Indo-Aryan *patʰás, from Proto-Indo-Iranian *patʰás, from Proto-Indo-European *pent- (“path”).

پتھر.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀧𑀢𑁆𑀣𑀭 m (patthara, “stone”) without cluster simplification, from Sanskrit प्रस्तर (prastara). Displaced Old Hindi पाथर (pāthara). Cognate with Punjabi پَتَّھر (pattahr). False cognate of Ancient Greek πέτρα (pétra).

پٹاخا.e: Hyperforeign form of پَٹاکا (paṭākā). Displaced پَٹاکا (paṭākā). Onomatopoeic, from پٹ (paṭ, “crack”).

senses.g: a sandalwood mark on the forehead
a member or supporter of the PML-N party
a Tory, or a politically conservative person.

پٹواری.e: Inherited from Sanskrit *पट्टपालिन् (*paṭṭapālin, literally “slab-keeper, cloth-keeper”). Equivalent to پَٹْوار (paṭvār) + ـی (-ī).

پٹول.e: Learned borrowing from Sanskrit पटोल (paṭola), ultimately from Proto-Dravidian *puḍala (“snake gourd, Trichosanthes anguina”). Doublet of پَرْوَل (parval), a tadbhava.

پٹھان.e: Inherited from Proto-New Indo-Aryan *𑀧𑀝𑁆𑀞𑀸𑀡 (*paṭṭhāṇa), from Proto-Middle Indo-Aryan *𑀧𑀱𑁆𑀝𑀸𑀦 (*paṣṭāna), borrowed from Proto-Iranian. Compare Punjabi پَٹھاݨ (paṭhāṇ), Odia ପଠାଣ (paṭhāṇa), Gujarati પઠાણ (paṭhāṇ), Marathi पठाण (paṭhāṇ), Bengali পাঠান (paṭhan). Related to Pashto پښتانۀ (paẍtânë); see there for more.

Inherited from Proto-New Indo-Aryan *𑀧𑀝𑁆𑀞𑀸𑀡 (*paṭṭhāṇa), from Proto-Middle Indo-Aryan *𑀧𑀱𑁆𑀝𑀸𑀦 (*paṣṭāna), borrowed from Proto-Iranian. Compare Punjabi پَٹھاݨ (paṭhāṇ), Odia ପଠାଣ (paṭhāṇa), Gujarati પઠાણ (paṭhāṇ), Marathi पठाण (paṭhāṇ), Bengali পাঠান (paṭhan). Related to Pashto پښتانۀ (paẍtânë); see there for more.

پچھم.e: Inherited from Middle Hindi پچھم (pchm /⁠paccham⁠/) (c. 1736),, a semi-learned borrowing from Sanskrit पश्चिम (paścima).
Inherited from Middle Hindi پچھم (pchm /⁠paccham⁠/) (c. 1736),, a semi-learned borrowing from Sanskrit पश्चिम (paścima).

پدر.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian پدر (pidar), from Middle Persian 𐭓𐭐𐭃 (pidar), from Old Persian 𐎱𐎡𐎫𐎠 (pitā), from Proto-Indo-European *ph₂tḗr. Doublet of پتا (pitā).

senses.g: alternative form of پودِینَہ (podīna, “mint, Mentha arvensis”)

ex.u: چَھت پَر
گَھر پَر

senses.g: Additional meanings as a compound postposition

پر.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀧𑀭𑀺 (pari), 𑀉𑀧𑁆𑀧𑀭𑀺 (uppari), from Sanskrit उपरि (upari, “above”).
Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀧𑀭𑀁 (paraṃ), from Sanskrit परम् (param, “after, but”), oblique of पर (para, “distant, further”).

پراپت.e: Borrowed from Sanskrit प्राप्त (prāpta), from प्राप् (prāp, “to reach, attain, obtain”), a compound of प्र- (pra-, prepositional prefix) + आप् (āp, “to reach, obtain”).

senses.g: alternative form of پُراتَم (purātam, “ancient, old, earliest”)

پراچین.e: Learned borrowing from Sanskrit प्राचीन (prācī́na) (with the sense "old" from Classical Sanskrit).

senses.g: old (ie. of an earlier time, in the past)

پرانا.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀧𑀼𑀭𑀸𑀡 (purāṇa) + Middle Indo-Aryan -𑀓- (-ka-), from Sanskrit पुराण (purāṇa).

پرتگال.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian پُرْتُگَال (purtugāl), influenced by English Portugal.

پرتگیا.e: Learned borrowing from Sanskrit प्रतिज्ञा (pratijñā). Doublet of پَیج (paij).

senses.g: Earth (the third planet from our Sun)

ex.u: جو چار کونے ہَیں پِرْتِھْوی کے رَہیں گے قائِم اُنْہیں کے دَم سے ہَوا ہَے اَب تَک نَہ کوئِی ہوگا جَہاں میں جَیسے یہ بِیر ہوںْگے
ex.r: jo cār kone ha͠i pirthivī ke rahẽ ge qāim unhẽ ke dam se havā hai ab tak na koī hogā jahā̃ mẽ jaise ye bīr hõge
ex.e: (please add an English translation of this quotation)

ex.u: پرچم کشائی

پرچم.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian پَرْچَم (parčam). First attested in c. 1510 as Middle Hindi پرچم (prcm /⁠parcam⁠/).

senses.g: oblique plural of پَرْدَہ (parda, “cover; curtain; cover”)

ex.u: چناچہ قرآن مجید میں اللہ تعالیٰ نے عورتوں پر پردہ فرض فرماتے ہوئے ارشاد فرمایا کہ
ex.e: Therefore, in the Holy Qur'an God Almighty makes the veil obligatory upon women stating that

ex.u:
ذبح کرنے میں کمی او ستم ایجاد نہ کر
رحم کر رحم کے پردے میں یہ بیداد نہ کر
ex.r:
ẕibaḥ karne meṉ kamī o sitam īyjād nah kar
raḥm kar raḥm ke parde meṉ yih bedād nah kar

پردہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian پرده (parda), ultimately from Proto-Indo-European *pleh₂- (“flat”).

پردہ دار.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian پرده دار۔ By surface analysis, دار (dār) + پَرْدَہ (parda).

senses.g: A woman who is concealed behind a curtain to avoid the gaze of others, particularly men and strangers.

پردہ نشین.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian پَرْدَه نَشِین (parda našīn).

senses.g: inflection of پَرْدَہ (parda, “cover; curtain; cover”): — direct plural
inflection of پَرْدَہ (parda, “cover; curtain; cover”): — oblique/vocative singular

پرستان.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian پَرِیسْتَان (parīstān), from پَرِی (parī) + ـسْتَان (-stān). By surface analysis, پَری (parī) + ـسْتان (-stān).

پرستش.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian پرستش (parastiš), from پرستیدن (parastīdan, “to worship”).

پرسوں.e: Inherited from Sanskrit परश्वस् (paraśvas), from पर (para, “next, following”) + श्वस् (śvas, “tomorrow”).

senses.g: Persia (former name of Iran, a country in Western Asia)

پرکھ.e: From Sanskrit पुरुष (púruṣa). Compare Punjabi پرکھ (purakh).

پرندہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian پَرَنْدَه (paranda). Equivalent to پَرِنْد (parind) + ـَہ (-a). First attested in c. 1645 as Middle Hindi پرندہ (prndh /⁠paranda⁠/).
Borrowed from Classical Persian پَرَنْدَه (paranda). Equivalent to پَرِنْد (parind) + ـَہ (-a). First attested in c. 1645 as Middle Hindi پرندہ (prndh /⁠paranda⁠/).

پرنی.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀧𑀭𑀺𑀡𑀻𑀤 (pariṇīda), from Sanskrit परिणीत (pariṇīta, “married”, adjective).

پرواز.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian پَرْوَاز (parwāz). First attested in c. 1611 as Middle Hindi پرواز (prvaz /⁠parvāz⁠/).

ex.u:
ہر آنکھ لہو ساگر ہے میاں ہر دل پتھر سناٹا ہے
یہ گنگا کس نے پاٹی ہے یہ پربت کس نے کاٹا ہے
har ā̃kh lahū sāgar hai miyā̃ har dil patthar sannāṭā hai
ex.r: ye gaṅgā kis ne pāṭī hai ye parbat kis ne kāṭā hai
ex.e: (please add an English translation of this quotation)

پروت.e: Learned borrowing from Sanskrit पर्वत (párvata), from Proto-Indo-Aryan *párwatas, from Proto-Indo-Iranian *párwatas, from Proto-Indo-European *pérwr̥ (“rock”).

پرورش.e: First attested in c. 1582 as Middle Hindi پرورش (parvariś), from Classical Persian پرورش (parvariš).

پرول.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit [Term?], from Sanskrit पटोल (paṭola), ultimately from Proto-Dravidian *puḍala. Doublet of پَٹول (paṭol), a tatsama. Cognate with Marathi पडवळ (paḍvaḷ).

پروہت.e: Borrowed from Sanskrit पुरोहित (purohita, “placed in front”).

پری.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian پری (parī), from Middle Persian plyk' (parīg).
